summary: Gruber dissects GM's decision to drop CarPlay from its EVs and hire former Apple cloud executive Mike Abbott to lead a new software unit, arguing the move is driven by unrealistic subscription revenue targets rather than user experience goals. He walks through back-of-the-envelope math showing GM's target of $20-25 billion in annual services revenue by 2030 is wildly implausible given their current $2 billion base and declining vehicle sales. He examines every possible revenue stream — maps, music, insurance, Google default placement payments, semi-autonomous driving — and finds none that close the gap. Gruber concludes this is an enormous bet against the primacy of the smartphone that no company has ever won, and predicts it will become an expensive boondoggle that drives buyers to competitors who still support CarPlay.
key_points: GM hired a cloud services executive, not a user-experience leader, signaling the move is about extracting subscription revenue rather than building great in-car software

Simple math shows growing from 4 million subscribers at ~$500/year to $20+ billion by 2030 would require roughly 30 million subscribers paying $55/month — numbers Gruber finds implausible given declining vehicle sales
Every service GM could charge for — maps, music, crash detection, insurance — is already available free or cheap on smartphones, so withholding CarPlay won't force adoption
Even a Google-pays-for-default-placement deal analogous to Apple's Safari search deal can't close the gap, since GM's vehicle fleet is tiny compared to Apple's billion-plus devices
Semi-autonomous driving (Super Cruise) is the only compelling subscription that phones can't replace, but it doesn't require dropping CarPlay to monetize
money_quotes: I don't look at this hire and think that GM is any more likely to come up with a CarPlay-quality interface for its own platform. Some back-of-the-envelope math on Barra's services revenue goals for GM makes it sound to me like Mike Abbott is being tasked with designing an in-dash slot machine.

What services could they offer that new car buyers would pay north of $50/month for? Maps and navigation? Everyone has that on their phones already. Music and podcasts? Everyone has that on their phones already.

GM executives might be dreaming that car buyers will pay GM for services they already get on their phones by not supporting CarPlay and Android Auto, but today's reality shows that people will just buy $5 mounts to attach their phones to their vehicles' dashboards.
It's an enormous bet against the primacy of the phone in people's lives, with no one yet, in any industry, ever having won a bet against the phone.

GM's $20–25 billion target for services revenue by 2030 feels like a Kendall Roy number — plucked out of thin air by someone just making up figures in an Excel spreadsheet until they get the SUM() result they're looking for.
Charging a subscription fee for Super Cruise doesn't require GM to drop support for CarPlay and Android Auto integration.
If every other automaker continues to support CarPlay and Android Auto, it just seems like this entire endeavor will turn into an expensive boondoggle that steers would-be GM buyers to other brands.
key_insight: GM's plan to drop CarPlay in pursuit of $20-25 billion in subscription revenue is built on fantasy math and a historically losing bet against the smartphone's dominance in people's lives.
